did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (specifically Vgg-m, Vgg-f, Vgg-verydeep-19, and CaffeNet) combined with the ABCD rule features as input to a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier to diagnose melanoma from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a new computational methodology combining the ABCD rule and pre-trained CNNs for melanoma diagnosis, builds a new database by joining two existing ones, performs extensive experimental testing with specific classifiers (MLP, SVM, KNN), and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 94.9% accuracy). Although it includes a related works section, its primary purpose is presenting original experimental validation of a proposed algorithm.

title: ABCD rule and pre-trained CNNs for melanoma diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes a computational method to assist in the diagnosis of melanoma from dermoscopic images by combining the ABCD rule features with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The researchers constructed a new database by joining two existing datasets to ensure diversity and evaluated the robustness of the method against segmentation errors. Features were selected using gain ratios and input into a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier. The proposed approach achieved an accuracy of 94.9% and a Kappa index of 89.2%, demonstrating excellent performance. The results indicate that combining multiple descriptors improves classification rates compared to using single descriptors or other state-of-the-art methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The study classifies images as melanoma or non-melanoma. The dataset (PH2 + DermIS) contained 406 images total, with the specific count of melanoma vs non-melanoma not explicitly broken down in the provided text, but the task is binary classification between these two.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The paper mentions 'non-melanoma' as the other class. Related works cited in the paper mention benign nevi, atypical nevi, and seborrheic keratosis as classes in other datasets, but the proposed method's dataset (PH2 + DermIS) is described generally as 'benign and melanoma categories' or 'healthy and unhealthy skin images'. The PH2 database typically contains melanoma, benign nevus, and dysplastic nevus. DermIS contains various benign lesions. The paper does not explicitly list the specific benign subtypes included in the PH2+DermIS union, only that they are 'non-melanoma'.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'melanoma' or 'non-melanoma' classes. The methodology combines ABCD rule features and pre-trained CNN features as input to a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and Kappa index on a confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database consisting of 200 images with RGB color system and resolution of 768x560 pixels. It includes binary masks of segmentation for each lesion.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.
use.validation: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.
use.testing: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.

datasets_used.name: DermIS Database
datasets_used.description: The Dermatology Information System, containing 206 dermoscopic images in RGB color system.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.
use.validation: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.
use.testing: Used as part of the combined dataset for training and testing the proposed method.

datasets_used.name: PH2 + DermIS Combined Database
datasets_used.description: A new database constructed by joining the PH2 and DermIS databases to validate the proposed methodology, containing a total of 406 dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the classifiers (MLP, SVM, KNN) and feature selection.
use.validation: Used for evaluating the robustness of the method and comparing descriptors.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance (accuracy, precision, recall, Kappa index) under normal and simulated segmentation error conditions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute classification performance metrics including precision, recall, accuracy, and Kappa index.
To measure inter-rater agreement or classification accuracy, accounting for chance, and to qualify performance quality (poor, moderate, good, very good, excellent).
To select features based on their significance for classification.
To combine outcomes from multiple classifiers (MLP, SVM, KNN) by averaging accuracy scores per class.
To evaluate the robustness of the proposed method under under-segmentation (10% decrease in ground-truth area) and over-segmentation (10% increase in ground-truth area) conditions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and the Kappa index, with qualitative interpretations of the Kappa values (e.g., 'excellent', 'very good') based on Landis and Koch's guidelines, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid descriptor combining ABCD rule features and pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), classified by a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP). Pre-trained CNNs used: AlexNet, CaffeNet, Vgg-f, Vgg-m, Vgg-s, Vgg-verydeep-16, and Vgg-verydeep-19.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on large natural image databases (ImageNet). Specifically, Vgg-verydeep-16 and Vgg-verydeep-19 were proposed at the ImageNet Challenge 2014. AlexNet was developed for ILSVRC 2010. CaffeNet was developed by BVLC. Vgg-f, Vgg-m, and Vgg-s were proposed by Chatfield et al. based on AlexNet.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported for the CNNs; PH2 database images were 768x560 pixels, DermIS images were RGB color system (dimensions not specified).

architecture_details.ABCD_rule: Calculates 5 features: asymmetry (1), irregular borders (2), color (1), diameter (1).
architecture_details.CNNs: Seven architectures used: AlexNet (8 layers: 5 conv with 5x5/7x7 filters, 3 FC, max-pooling), CaffeNet (5 conv, 3 FC, different pooling/normalization order than AlexNet), Vgg-f/m/s (5 conv, 3 FC, based on AlexNet), Vgg-verydeep-16/19 (many layers with 3x3 filters). CNNs return 4,096 features each.
architecture_details.feature_vector: Concatenation of ABCD (5 features) and selected CNN features. Total initial attributes: 16,389. Optimal subset: 820 attributes (5% of total).
architecture_details.classifier: MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP). Hidden layer composed of two neurons. Activation function: approximate sigmoid. Loss function: quadratic error. Learning method: back-propagation.

training_details.feature_selection: Gain ratio information algorithm (Ranker method) used to select top 5% most relevant attributes (820 out of 16,389).
training_details.other_classifiers_tested: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with linear kernel, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) with k=1, Random Forest (100 trees, unlimited depth), Ensemble of MLP+SVM+KNN (average voting).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble of MLP, SVM, and KNN
preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation of skin lesions using binary masks provided by the PH2 and DermIS databases
Conversion of images from RGB color model to HSV color model
Generation of images by summing the Saturation (S) and Value (V) bands of the HSV model for feature extraction
conclusions: The proposed computational method, which combines ABCD rule features with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) features, achieved an accuracy of 94.9% and a Kappa index of 89.2% (classified as 'excellent') for melanoma diagnosis.
Combining multiple descriptors (hybrid descriptor) significantly improved classification performance compared to using single descriptors or other state-of-the-art methods.
The optimal feature subset consisted of only 5% of the total attributes (820 out of 16,389), selected using gain ratios, demonstrating efficient feature selection.
The MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier generally performed better than SVM, KNN, and Random Forest classifiers, achieving the highest Kappa indices across most descriptors.

The proposed method demonstrated robustness against segmentation errors; it maintained 'very good' and 'excellent' Kappa indices even under simulated under-segmentation and over-segmentation conditions.
Information from regions close to the lesion edge is essential for skin lesion classification, as under-segmentation resulted in lower success rates (87.0%) compared to over-segmentation (90.8%).
Using a combined database (PH2 + DermIS) with diverse images validated the methodology's ability to handle distinct database characteristics.
important_remarks: The study relies on a combined dataset of only 406 images (PH2 + DermIS), which limits the scale of external validation compared to larger public datasets like ISIC.

The proposed method achieved 94.9% accuracy, but the paper acknowledges that Cavalcanti et al. [8] achieved higher accuracy (98.66%) on a smaller, single-database dataset (152 images), suggesting potential overfitting or lack of generalizability in the proposed method.
The paper simulates segmentation errors (10% under/over-segmentation) to test robustness rather than using a dedicated segmentation algorithm, which may not reflect real-world clinical variability.

Feature selection was performed using gain ratios, retaining only 5% (820 features) of the total 16,389 attributes; this aggressive reduction might discard clinically relevant but less statistically significant features.
The comparison with state-of-the-art methods in Table 6 is uneven, as many cited works used different databases, image counts, or evaluation metrics, making direct performance comparisons difficult.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed method and others, relying instead on qualitative interpretations of the Kappa index.

The dataset composition is not fully transparent; while PH2 has known class distributions, the specific breakdown of benign vs. melanoma classes in the combined PH2+DermIS dataset is not explicitly detailed in the results section.
The method uses pre-trained CNNs (AlexNet, CaffeNet, Vgg-f/m/s, Vgg-verydeep-16/19) which were trained on natural images (ImageNet), not medical images, potentially introducing domain bias.
